name: Pinaka
description: The Pinaka is an ancient and divine bow of immense power, prominently featured in Hindu mythology. Revered as one of the most potent weapons wielded by the gods, it is primarily associated with Lord Shiva, the destroyer and transformer within the Hindu trinity. Forged by the divine architect Vishwakarma, the Pinaka is not merely a weapon but a symbol of Shiva's cosmic power and his ability to maintain balance in the universe. Its construction is said to have involved celestial materials and divine energies, imbuing it with extraordinary strength and accuracy. The bow is described as being capable of launching arrows that could cleave mountains and evaporate oceans. The mere sound of its string being drawn was enough to instill fear in the hearts of even the most formidable adversaries. Over time, the Pinaka was eventually passed down, or given away, to other powerful figures, though it always retained its connection to Shiva's essence and represents his destructive and protective capabilities.
subtitle: Shiva's divine bow.
origin: Forged by Vishwakarma with celestial materials and divine energy.
currentLocation: Broken; fragments of the bow are said to be scattered, but the location is unknown and debated.

keyMyths.title: Shiva's Use of Pinaka During Daksha's Yagna
keyMyths.description: When Daksha, Sati's father, insulted Shiva during a grand yagna (sacrifice), Sati immolated herself. Enraged, Shiva, wielding the Pinaka, unleashed his fury, destroying Daksha's yagna and wreaking havoc. This episode showcases the bow's destructive power and Shiva's wrathful nature when provoked. The bow's arrows shattered the sacrificial altars and scattered the assembled deities.

keyMyths.title: The Breaking of the Pinaka at Sita's Swayamvara
keyMyths.description: King Janaka held a swayamvara (ceremony where a princess chooses her husband) for his daughter Sita, stipulating that the suitor must string the Pinaka, which was a family heirloom received from Shiva. Many kings and princes failed. Rama, the incarnation of Vishnu, effortlessly strung the bow, and in doing so, broke it, fulfilling a prophecy and winning Sita's hand. This signifies Rama's divine strength.

keyMyths.title: Parashurama and the Pinaka
keyMyths.description: In some versions of the Ramayana, after Rama breaks the Pinaka, Parashurama, another avatar of Vishnu, arrives, enraged by the breaking of Shiva's bow. He challenges Rama, testing his strength and divinity. This encounter serves to establish Rama's superiority and confirms his divine nature as an avatar of Vishnu, surpassing even the power of Parashurama.

sources.author: Vettam Mani
sources.work: Puranic Encyclopaedia: A Comprehensive Dictionary With Special Reference to the Epic and Puranic Literature
sources.description: Provides detailed information about the Pinaka, its wielders, and its significance in various Puranic texts.
